eli3: Lagrange multipliers solve constrained optimization: maximize f(x) subject to g(x) = 0. The trick is to create a new function L = f(x) - λg(x) and find where its gradient is zero.

Geometrically, at the optimum, the gradient of f (direction of steepest increase) must be perpendicular to the constraint surface. This means ∇f and ∇g are parallel: ∇f = λ∇g. The multiplier λ measures how much the objective changes if we relax the constraint.

Think of walking along a path (constraint) looking for the highest point. You're at a peak when the uphill direction is perpendicular to the path — any step along the path neither climbs nor descends.

whenWhy.title: Support Vector Machines
whenWhy.body: SVM optimization: maximize margin subject to classification constraints. Lagrange multipliers (dual variables) become support vector weights. The dual problem is often easier to solve and reveals sparsity (only support vectors matter).

whenWhy.title: Portfolio optimization with constraints
whenWhy.body: Maximize return subject to risk budget, or minimize risk subject to return target. Constraints include: sum of weights = 1, no shorting (w ≥ 0), sector limits. Lagrange multipliers give shadow prices of constraints.

whenWhy.title: Maximum entropy and exponential families
whenWhy.body: Find maximum entropy distribution subject to moment constraints. Lagrange multipliers become natural parameters of the exponential family. This connects to maximum likelihood estimation and variational inference.

whenWhy.title: Physics and mechanics
whenWhy.body: Principle of least action with constraints (holonomic constraints in Lagrangian mechanics). Forces of constraint appear as Lagrange multipliers. Duality between force and displacement constraints.

deepDiveIntro: To optimize an objective subject to an equality constraint, form the Lagrangian by subtracting the multiplier times the constraint from the objective. Setting all partial derivatives to zero gives the stationarity conditions: the constraint is satisfied, and the objective and constraint gradients point in the same direction. The multiplier measures how sensitive the optimal value is to a small relaxation of the constraint.

With multiple equality constraints, one multiplier is assigned to each, and the Lagrangian sums all of them. Each multiplier is the rate at which the optimal objective value changes when that constraint is loosened slightly. Inequality constraints are handled by the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker conditions, which add the rule that a multiplier must be zero whenever the corresponding constraint is not active.

deepDive.heading: The Lagrangian
deepDive.body: The Lagrangian combines the original objective with the constraint into one expression, so a constrained problem can be analyzed using stationary-point ideas. Solving the resulting conditions finds points that satisfy the constraint and balance the objective against it. The multiplier records how sensitive the optimal value is to small relaxations of the constraint.

deepDive.heading: Geometric Interpretation
deepDive.body: At a constrained optimum, you cannot improve the objective by moving along the feasible surface. That means the objective's steepest direction must line up with directions that are normal to the constraint surface. Geometrically, the objective contour just touches the constraint at the solution.

deepDive.heading: Multiple Constraints
deepDive.body: With several equality constraints, you assign one multiplier to each constraint and combine them all in the Lagrangian. The optimality condition says the objective gradient must be explained by the normals generated by all constraint gradients together. This is the natural extension of the single-constraint picture.

deepDive.heading: Sensitivity Interpretation
deepDive.body: The multiplier also has an economic interpretation. It tells you how much the best achievable objective value would change if the constraint were relaxed slightly. That is why multipliers are often called shadow prices in economics and optimization.

deepDive.heading: Inequality Constraints (KKT)
deepDive.body: Inequality constraints require an extra rule: only constraints that are tight at the solution should carry a positive multiplier. If a constraint is loose, its multiplier must be zero. These conditions, together with stationarity and feasibility, form the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker framework used throughout modern optimization.

takeaways: Lagrangian L = f(x) + λg(x) converts constrained to unconstrained problem.
Optimum occurs where ∇f = -λ∇g (gradients parallel, contours tangent).
Multiplier λ = shadow price = rate of change of optimal value vs constraint.
Multiple constraints: one λᵢ per constraint, sum in Lagrangian.
KKT conditions generalize to inequality constraints with complementary slackness.

edgeCases.q: What if the constraint qualification fails?
edgeCases.a: Constraint qualification (e.g., LICQ: ∇gᵢ linearly independent) ensures Lagrange multipliers exist. Without it, optimum may exist but no λ satisfies conditions. Example: minimize x subject to x³ = 0. At x=0, ∇g = 0, so ∇f = λ∇g has no solution with finite λ.

For convex problems (convex f, convex feasible set from convex gᵢ), KKT conditions are sufficient for global optimality. For non-convex problems, KKT points can be local minima, maxima, or saddles — second-order conditions needed to classify.

edgeCases.q: How do I handle both equality and inequality constraints?
edgeCases.a: Include both with separate multipliers: L = f + λᵀh + μᵀg where h(x)=0 are equalities and g(x)≤0 are inequalities. KKT applies to inequalities (μ ≥ 0, μᵢgᵢ = 0). Equalities have free λ (can be positive or negative).

sources.label: Nocedal & Wright — Numerical Optimization (2nd ed.), Ch. 12
